coredata.dc:title: Self-calibrating sensor with logic gate operation for anthrax biomarker based on nanoscaled bimetallic lanthanoid MOF
coredata.dc:creator: Othong, Jintana
Boonmak, Jaursup
Kielar, Filip
Hadsadee, Sarinya
Jungsuttiwong, Siriporn
Youngme, Sujittra
coredata.dc:description: The bacillus anthracis is one of toxic bacterial spores that can cause human anthrax infections. In this effort, the nanoscaled bimetallic lanthanoid metal-organic framework (Ln-MOF), namely, [Tb0.43Eu1.57(1,4-phda)3(H2O)](H2O)2 was synthesized by tuning the mole ratio of Tb3+ and Eu3+ as a ratiometric luminescent sensor for anthrax biomarker (dipicolinic acid, DPA). The detection limits of this DPA sensor are as low as 5.9 nM in methanol and 0.17 μM in water which are lower than human infectious doses. This sensor can detect DPA via turn-on process with broad ranges at either low concentration (0−800 nM) in methanol or high concentration (20−100 μM) in an aqueous medium. Moreover, the bimetallic Ln-MOF’s emission colors in the presence of DPA are changed from red-orange to orange-yellow in methanol, and red-orange to green in water that can be easily seen in solution and film by naked-eyes. In addition, one-to-two logic gate operation for monitoring DPA concentration levels of this sensor has been developed. The luminescent probe is also applied for DPA detection in real tap-water and urine samples with good performances.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
Self-calibration
Anthrax biomarker
Logic gate operation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The photoluminescence sensing of DPA biomarker
Structural character of [Tb2-xEux(1,4-phda)3(H2O)](H2O)2; x = 0.73 for 1, 1.22 for 2, 1.57 for 3
Self-calibrating luminescent detection of DPA and logic gate operation
Determination of DPA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Luminescent spectra of 3 under various DPA concentrations in methanol (a) and water (b) Plot of the relationship of I544/I617 emission spectra of 3 upon DPA concentrations of 0-800 nM (c) and 20-100 μM (d) upon excitation at 260 nm.
Luminescent photographs of 3 in the solution phase and film without DPA addition (middle), 800 nM DPA addition (right) and 100 μM DPA addition (left) under UV light and daylight.

Diagram of normalized intensity of I544 (Tb3+) and I617 (Eu3+) toward 100 μM DPA (a) and 20-80 μM DPA (c). The truth table of one-to-two logic gate (b) and varied concentration of DPA from 0 to 80 (d).
The luminescent sensing mechanism of 3 after DPA addition.

Simplified luminescent diagram of the ligand-to-metal/analyte-to-metal energy transfers and the energy transfer between the lowest excited states 5D4 and 5D0 of the Tb3+ and Eu3+ centers. (S0 is the ground state of the ligand/analyte; S1 and T1 are the singlet state and triplet state of the ligand/analyte, respectively).
Luminescent response of 3 toward interference species in methanol (a) and aqueous media (b) upon excitation at 260 nm.
Performance comparison between various types of luminescent probes employed for the detection of DPA.
Determination of DPA in real samples.

head.ce:title: Self-calibrating sensor with logic gate operation for anthrax biomarker based on nanoscaled bimetallic lanthanoid MOF

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Organic, Electronic, and Alternative Energy
Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCenter for Organic, Electronic, and Alternative Energy, Department of Chemistry and Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, Ubon Ratchathani 34190, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The self-calibrating sensor based on bimetallic Ln-MOF shows low detection limit and high selectivity for anthrax biomarker. The one-to-two logic gate has been developed for monitoring DPA.

The bacillus anthracis is one of toxic bacterial spores that can cause human anthrax infections. In this effort, the nanoscaled bimetallic lanthanoid metal-organic framework (Ln-MOF), namely, [Tb0.43Eu1.57(1,4-phda)3(H2O)](H2O)2 was synthesized by tuning the mole ratio of Tb3+ and Eu3+ as a ratiometric luminescent sensor for anthrax biomarker (dipicolinic acid, DPA). The detection limits of this DPA sensor are as low as 5.9 nM in methanol and 0.17 μM in water which are lower than human infectious doses. This sensor can detect DPA via turn-on process with broad ranges at either low concentration (0−800 nM) in methanol or high concentration (20−100 μM) in an aqueous medium. Moreover, the bimetallic Ln-MOF’s emission colors in the presence of DPA are changed from red-orange to orange-yellow in methanol, and red-orange to green in water that can be easily seen in solution and film by naked-eyes. In addition, one-to-two logic gate operation for monitoring DPA concentration levels of this sensor has been developed. The luminescent probe is also applied for DPA detection in real tap-water and urine samples with good performances.

ce:section.ce:para: Anthrax is one of acute diseases caused by inhalation of the bacillus anthracis spores concentration higher than 104 and it infects both humans and warm-blooded animals [1–3]. Bacillus anthracis spores have high adaptability for extreme environment, therefore the development of high efficiency method for ultrasensitive detection is important to prevent bioterrorism and anthrax disease control. It is well-known that calcium dipicolinate (CaDPA) is a major constituent of bacillus anthracis spores, so dipicolinic acid (DPA) acts as a biomarker for anthrax [4,5]. There are many methods for DPA determination including traditional biological and chemical methods [6,7]. However, the biological methods have several limitations such as expensive cost, complicated treatment, long operating period and professional analysis [8,9]. Recently, the optical sensing methods based on luminescent technique have received attention for DPA detection because it provides low cost, rapid response, simple operating, non-invasive and high sensitivity [10–12]. However, most of reported luminescent sensor for DPA detection involve “turn off” signal of a single emission which suffers from discriminative color detection of DPA, low selectivity and low precision [13]. Therefore, the preparation of the self-calibrating sensor with the internal reference is vital for reducing interruption from the interferences, and enhancement of reliability without the instrument calibration [14–17]. Moreover, the luminescent sensor can be utilized as Boolean logic gate operation which transmits binary and multivalued information like human brains via the combination between optical/chemical inputs and outputs [18,19]. Luminescent-based logic gates have received great attention to design smart-materials for using in many fields such as biomedical diagnostics, drug delivery and so on [20,21]. Therefore, the design of a practical logic device based on luminescent sensor can provide a facile method for monitoring DPA levels in humans.

Up to now, several luminescent probes have been prepared and designed including quantum dots, [22,23] transition metal-based coordination polymers [24,25], and organic dyes [26,27]. However, these materials have many disadvantages such as photobleaching, high toxicity and interruption of interference [28,29]. Among the luminescent probes, bimetallic lanthanoid metal-organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) have attracted great interest because (i) they show sharp emission bands, high color purity, long life-time and large Stokes shifts [30–32], (ii) the tunable luminescence of Ln-MOFs can be applied for dual emission through mixed Ln(III) centers to get rid of the fluctuate emission from the interferences [33–35] (iii) the Ln(III) ions can interact with DPA resulting in the changeable emission through the energy transfer process. Recently, some MOFs were applied for detecting anthrax biomarker. However, there are still some limitations including narrow linear range for detection and unusable detection in aqueous media for the practical sensing performance in real samples. In addition, the utilizing of nanoscale luminescent probes for biosensor has been in increasing attention [36,37] due to clear improvement of their sensitivity. Apart from the use for the solution-based detection, the film-based luminescent probe is more easy-to-use for preliminary screening [38,39].

Herein, the nanoscaled bimetallic lanthanoid MOFs [Tb2-xEux(1,4-phda)3(H2O)](H2O)2; x = 0.73, 1.22, 1.57 have been synthesized under simple sonochemical reaction for DPA sensing via efficient dual-emission process. The performance of [Tb0.43Eu1.57(1,4-phda)3(H2O)](H2O)2 as a sensor shows high selectivity, high sensitivity and rapid detection. In the presence of DPA with appropriate concentration, the discriminative luminescence of both solution and film was observed from red-orange to orange-yellow and from red-orange to green emission color in methanol and water, respectively. Moreover, the colorimetric logic gate for monitoring DPA levels based on this sensor has been developed. The detection of DPA in tap-water and urine samples was also evaluated.

All chemical substances including 1,4-phenylenediacetic acid (97 %), salicylic acid, isophthalic acid, 3-hydroxybenzoic acid, aminobenzoic acid, benzoic acid, dipicolinic acid, metal salts and solvents were purchased commercially and used without further purification.

The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data was recorded on a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation in the 2Ѳ range of 5−50° with scan rate of 0.5 s/steps. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of bimetallic Ln-MOFs were measured by Hitachi S-4800. The mole ratios of bimetallic Ln-MOFs were determined by ICP-AES. The lifetime of bimetallic Ln-MOFs were measured on a Horiba Scientific Fluoromax 4 spectrometer equipped with pulsed Xenon light. The samples were placed under a quartz glass slide on the solid sample holder and the angle between the excitation light and the sample was set to 30 degrees. Measurement and analysis were performed using the Fluorescence software package. The photoluminescence spectra in solid state and solution phase were measured by RF-6000 spectrofluorophotometer. Mass spectra were analyzed by Bruker Impact II Q-TOF.

The bimetallic Ln-MOFs were synthesized by modified method from previous literature [40]. Eu(NO3)3∙5H2O (x mmol), Tb(NO3)3∙5H2O (2-x mmol) and sodium acetate (0.2 mmol) (x =0.001 mmol for 1, 0.005 mmol for 2 and 0.01 mmol for 3) were dissolved in aqueous solution (6 mL). Then, 1,4-phenylenediacetic acid (1,4-phda) (0.6 mmol) in methanol 8 ml was added into the mixture under sonication for 30 min. The white powders were obtained and washed with methanol and water. Then, the products were dried at room temperature. Yields: (62 %, 55 % and 32 % for 1, 2 and 3 based on Tb3+ salt).

(PVA) (0.3 g) was dissolved in water (3 mL) and heated 90 °C for 1 h. Then, the bimetallic Ln-MOF (3) was suspended in an ethanol and slowly dropped into PVA solution. This solution was stirred for 1 h and it was slowly cast onto glass plate. Finally, PVA/Ln-MOF film was obtained after drying at room temperature.

The electronic structure calculations for both the ground state and the excited state of 1,4-phda were performed with Gaussian 09 program. The optimized ground state is calculated by DFT at B3LYP/6−311 G(d,p) level. The excited state structure is optimized by TDDFT/B3LYP/6−311 G(d,p) level. The emission spectra were calculated by TDDFT with the same method of optimization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photoluminescence sensing of DPA biomarker
ce:section.ce:para: The compounds 1–3 (1 mg) were dispersed in methanol (50 mL) and sonicated for 30 min to form the suspension. This suspension (2 mL) was transferred to 5 ml volumetric flask, then an incremental DPA concentration was adjusted to give the final concentration to 100−800 nM. The luminescent spectra of suspension were measured by monitoring at 544 nm for Tb-MOF, 617 nm for Eu-MOF, and I544/I617 for bimetallic Ln-MOFs with an excitation at 260 nm. The DPA sensing performance in water media was synthesized in the same manner as in methanol. Bimetallic Ln-MOFs (1 mg) were dispersed in water (50 mL) instead of methanol and adjusted the final concentration of DPA to 20−100 μM. The selectivity test was performed by using benzoic acid, aminobenzoic acid, 3-hydroxybenzoic acid, salicylic acid and isophthalic acid, Mg(II), Ca(II), Fe(II), Cu(II) and Na(II) as interferences in methanol. The same interferences were used in water for the selectivity test, except that Fe(II) was replaced by Fe(III) due to oxidation of Fe(II) in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural character of [Tb2-xEux(1,4-phda)3(H2O)](H2O)2; x = 0.73 for 1, 1.22 for 2, 1.57 for 3
ce:section.ce:para: The PXRD patterns of bimetallic Ln-MOFs are identical to that of known bulk phase of previously reported [40] (Figure S1), confirming that the bimetallic Ln-MOFs are isostructural. The Tb3+/Eu3+ contents in bimetallic Ln-MOFs were determined by ICP-AES. The mole ratios of Tb3+/Eu3+ are 1.74 for 1, 0.64 for 2, and 0.27 for 3. The morphology of bimetallic Ln-MOFs was investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) that shows round-shaped nanocrystals with the average size of 40−70 nm (Figure S2). The suspension of bimetallic Ln-MOFs shows good dispersion in methanol and water confirmed by the Tyndall effect (Figure S2).

The solid-state luminescent properties of single and bimetallic Ln-MOFs were examined. Upon excitation at 370 nm, Tb-MOF shows four characteristic emissions at 488, 544, 583 and 619 nm, which are assigned to 5D4 → 7FJ (J = 6−3) transitions of Tb3+ ion (Figure S3) while the characteristic emissions of Eu-MOF are observed at 590, 617, 648 and 696 nm with excitation at 465 nm, corresponding to 5D0 → 7FJ (J = 1–4) transitions of Eu3+ ion (Figure S4). The disappearance of ligand-based emission suggests that the energy transfer from ligand to Ln(III) ions is efficient as confirmed by DFT calculation (Figures S5-S13 and Table S1).

For bimetallic Ln-MOFs (1–3), all compounds were excited at 380 nm showing the characteristic emission of both Tb3+ and Eu3+ ions. When increasing the content of Eu3+ in 1–3, the green-emission of Tb3+ is gradually decreased while red-emission of Eu3+ is increased (Figure S13). This is attributed to the energy transfer from Tb3+ ion to Eu3+ ion confirmed by the double exponential decay plot for Tb-MOF (monitored at 544 nm), Eu-MOF (monitored at 617 nm) and compounds 1–3 (monitored at 544 and 617 nm). The average lifetimes of all compounds were calculated according to this equation:
(1) τav = (A1τ2 f + A2τ2 s) / (A1τf + A2τs)
(τf and τs stand for the fast and slow decay of luminescent lifetime whereas A1 and A2 are the exponential factors of the fitted curve).

The average lifetimes of 5D4 (Tb3+) and 5D0 (Eu3+) in Tb-MOF and Eu-MOF are observed at 1.88 and 0.94 ms (Table S2). Obviously, compounds 1–3 reveal that the lifetime of 5D4 (Tb3+) is shorter than Tb-MOF whereas the lifetime of 5D0 (Eu3+) is longer than Eu-MOF (Table S2). This phenomenon implies that the energy is transferred from Tb3+ ion to Eu3+ ion (Figure S13). The efficiency of energy transfer (ηT) from Tb3+ ion to Eu3+ ion is calculated as following equation:
(2) ηT = 1-τ /τ0

(τ0 is the lifetime of Tb3+ in single Ln-MOF and τ is the lifetime of Tb3+ in compounds 1–3. The ηT for compounds 1–3 are estimated to be 21.81 %, 40.96 % and 64.89 % indicating high efficiency of energy transfer (Figure S14).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-calibrating luminescent detection of DPA and logic gate operation
ce:section.ce:para:
To prevent issues and enhance the reliability in DPA determination, the compounds 1–3 were examined through ratiometric luminescent detection. To optimize the DPA sensing medium, the luminescent intensity of compounds 1–3 in methanol, ethanol, acetone, N,N-dimethylformamide and water was initially investigated (Figure S15). The result indicates that the highest luminescent intensities in methanol and water media were observed, thus both solvents were used for DPA detection. The compounds 1–3 (1 mg) were dispersed in methanol (50 mL) and measured (λexcitation =260 nm) upon various DPA concentration from 0−800 nM. The variable emission spectra of compounds 1–3 are shown in Figs. 1 a and S16. After adding DPA, the emission spectra of all compounds are changed. For compound 1, both Tb3+ and Eu3+ emission intensity are initially enhanced and then quenched along with increased DPA concentration and become enhanced again at high DPA concentration (Figure S16a). However, the luminescent emission intensity of Tb3+ and Eu3+ is gradually increased in compound 2 whereas that of compound 3 is increased markedly (Figures S16b and 1a). To evaluate the sensing performance of 3 for the practical uses, the luminescent response of 3 in aqueous solution was also analyzed upon added 0−100 μM of DPA (Fig. 1b). The luminescent intensity of Tb3+ is dominantly increased together with that of Eu3+ being slightly increased. As compound 3 shows similar results in both methanol and water, compound 3 can act as a self-calibrating luminescent sensor for DPA. Based on the ratiometric luminescent components (Tb emission at 544 nm and Eu emission at 617 nm), the relationship between emission intensity ratio I544/I617 against DPA concentration is plotted in Figs. 1c-d. The result indicates that the working range exhibits linear response with DPA concentration in the region from 0−800 nM in methanol and 20−100 μM in water. The linear correlation can be fitted with these equations:
(3) In methanol: I544/I617 = 0.0003*C[DPA] + 0.6845 (R2 = 0.9963)
(4) In water: I544/I617 = 0.0448*C[DPA] + 5.6103 (R2 = 0.9929)

In order for convenient, easy and non-invasive detection, the compound 3 can additionally be applied as a film based luminescent probe for DPA determination (Fig. 2). The structure of this film based material was confirmed by PXRD technique (Figure S17). The PXRD of film based bimetallic Ln-MOF matched well with that of 3 (Figure S17) suggesting that they are isostructural. In the presence of DPA, the luminescent color switched from red-orange to orange-yellow in methanol and red-orange to green in water under UV light via naked-eyes in the same manner as the solution phase (Fig. 2). However, the change in color of film materials takes about an hour to complete.

This DPA sensor in the solution phase can also be utilized as a Boolean logic-gate operation for monitoring DPA concentration levels. The single input and dual emission intensities of I544 (Tb3+) and I617 (Eu3+) as double outputs generating one-to-two logic gate were studied. As the input signal, the absence and presence of DPA were defined namely, “0” and “1”, respectively. The normalized luminescent intensities of I544 (Tb3+) and I617 (Eu3+) were taken as an output signal with a threshold value of 0.5 (Fig. 3 a). The intensities of I544 (Tb3+) and I617 (Eu3+) show higher and lower values than 0.5, which were defined as binary “1” and “0” in the output signals, respectively (Fig. 3a and b). In the absence of DPA, the output (0,0) is observed. After sensing, the luminescent intensity of I544 (Tb3+) dominantly increases producing output (1,0). The truth table in logic gate strategy is given in Fig. 3b. Moreover, the identification of direct detection and varied concentration of DPA levels were performed by placing the output signal into two logic types: “NO” (0,0) and “YES” (1,0). Five concentrations from 0 to 80 μM were set as input signal and the intensity of I544 (Tb3+) and I617 (Eu3+) as output were normalized (Fig. 3c). When the concentration was lower than 60 μM, the NO gate appears whereas the YES gate is triggered by higher concentration of DPA (≥60 μM). Thus, the binary codes were generated for DPA levels as follows: the output (0,0) which reflects no DPA or low DPA concentration, and output (1,0) which reflects high concentration of DPA (Fig. 3d). This binary codes can serve the identification of DPA level for human being (<60 μM) and risk for infection (≥60 μM). Each concentration of DPA also displays the discriminative colors with three groups: 0−20 μM (orange), 20−40 μM (yellow-green) and 40−80 μM (green).

The relationship between Tb3+ emission intensity of compound 3 and DPA existence was confirmed by monitoring at λemission =544 nm under λexcitation =260 nm. In methanol, the emission intensity of Tb3+ in compound 3 is very weak with the absence of DPA and constant in its initial step. When each addition of 250 nM DPA into the bimetallic Ln-MOF suspension is made, the Tb3+ emission intensity is rapidly increased within 30 s and becomes stable after 90 s (Figure S19). In water, the intensity of Tb3+ increases immediately after adding 100 μM of DPA for each time and reaches equilibrium in less than 60 s. This confirms that the compound 3 exhibits fast response for DPA detection in methanol and water solution and it is potentially used in the on-line DPA monitoring.

The selectivity is an important factor for a chemosensor. Herein, the luminescent response of bimetallic Ln-MOFs (3) under various interfering species including benzoic acid, aminobenzoic acid, 3-hydroxybenzoic acid, salicylic acid and isophthalic acid that are structurally related to DPA (Figure S20), were examined. Also, Mg(II), Ca(II), Fe(II), Fe(III), Cu(II) and Na(II) as common cellular ions were studied for the selectivity test. Remarkably, the luminescent intensity (I544/I617) of compound 3 in methanol and water are negligibly changed even the presence of these interferences and it can provide a prominent luminescent change after DPA addition (Fig. 6 ). The high selectivity of DPA over its analogs is attributed to high coordination ability of DPA with Ln(III) ions in tridentate chelating fashion [41,50–52]. Therefore, the compound 3 reveals high selectivity for DPA in both methanol and water systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DPA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore application of 3 towards DPA in real biological and environmental samples, various concentrations of DPA were added in tap-water and urine samples and then measured. The emission intensity ratios I544/I617 were converted to the concentration by Eq. 4 (Table 2 ). The recovery of DPA in tap-water and urine samples are between 98.48 % and 107.98 % which revealed good recovery values and the small relative standard deviation (RSD < 0.97 %, n = 3). Therefore, compound 3 can provide ratiometric luminescent sensor for detecting DPA in the real samples with high reliability.

The nanoscaled bimetallic Ln-MOF [Tb0.43Eu1.57(1,4-phda)3(H2O)](H2O)2 (3) as a ratiometric luminescent sensor for detecting DPA, anthrax biomarker, was successfully developed. Bimetallic Ln-MOF based on dual luminescence exhibits high reliability and accuracy as a result of reduced interruption from external factors compared to a single emission source. This sensor possesses fast response and high selectivity for DPA determination. Besides, LOD values of DPA detection are as low as 5.9 nM in methanol and 0.17 μM in water with wide linear range response at low concentrations (0−800 nM, in methanol) and higher concentrations (20−100 μM, in water). This compound also visually detects DPA through a luminescent color change from red-orange to orange-yellow in methanol and red-orange to green in water through “turn on” method in both the film and solution phase. Furthermore, this sensor was successfully used to detect DPA with good recovery and accuracy in real tap-water and urine samples. The one-to-two logic gate was successfully constructed. The output signals were observed as NO (0,0) for no DPA, and low concentration of DPA and YES (1,0) for high concentration of DPA. This strategy can help for monitoring the DPA levels to prevent harm in human health (<60 μM).

Jintana Othong: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Jaursup Boonmak: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Validation, Investigation. Filip Kielar: Investigation. Sarinya Hadsadee: Software. Siriporn Jungsuttiwong: Software. Sujittra Youngme: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding for this work is provided by The Thailand Research Fund: Grant No. BRG5980014, and the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH–CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128156.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional uniform nanoparticles composed of a magnetite nanocrystal core and a mesoporous silica shell for magnetic resonance and fluorescence imaging and for drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Kim, H.S. Kim, N. Lee, T. Kim, H. Kim, T. Yu, I.C. Song, W.K. Moon, T. Hyeon, Multifunctional uniform nanoparticles composed of a magnetite nanocrystal core and a mesoporous silica shell for magnetic resonance and fluorescence imaging and for drug delivery, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 120 (2008) 8566-8569. https://doi.org/10.1002/ange.200802469.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembly of magnetically recoverable ratiometric Cu2+ fluorescent sensor and adsorbent

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Lu, F. Teng, Y. Liu, L. Lu, C. Chen, J. Lei, L. Wang, J. Zhang, Self-assembly of magnetically recoverable ratiometric Cu2+ fluorescent sensor and adsorbent, RSC Adv. 4 (2014) 18660-18667. https://doi.org/10.1039/C4RA01599A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A valuable visual colorimetric and electrochemical biosensor for porphyrin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.-K. Oh, Y.S. Jeong, J. Song, J. Jang, A valuable visual colorimetric and electrochemical biosensor for porphyrin, Biosens. Bioelectron. 29 (2011) 172-177. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2011.06.043.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Performance assessment of three commercial assays for direct detection of bacillus anthracis spores

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. King, V. Luna, A. Cannons, J. Cattani, P. Amuso, Performance assessment of three commercial assays for direct detection of bacillus anthracis spores, J. Clin. Microbiol. 41 (2003) 3454-3455. https://doi.org/10.1128/JCM.41.7.3454-3455.2003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile bi-phase synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2 core–shell nanoparticles with tunable film thicknesses

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zou, Y.-G. Peng, Y.-Y. Tang, A facile bi-phase synthesis of Fe3O4@SiO2 core–shell nanoparticles with tunable film thicknesses, RSC Adv. 4 (2014) 9693-9700. https://doi.org/10.1039/C3RA47043A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fast and sensitive detection of an anthrax biomarker using SERS-based solenoid microfluidic sensor

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical DNA sensor for anthrax toxin activator gene atxA-detection of PCR amplicons

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jintana Othong has graduated a Ph.D. in Inorganic Chemistry from Khon Kaen University, Thailand in 2018 and is currently post-doctoral fellow in Department of Chemistry at Khon Kaen University in Thailand. She is interested in design and synthesis of metal-organic frameworks and their applications such as in ion exchange/separation, catalysts/photocatalysis, luminescence and colorimetric sensing.

Jaursup boonmak received his Ph.D. in Chemistry from Khon Kaen University, Thailand in 2012. He is currently an assistant professor at Department of Chemistry in the same university. His current research focuses on the synthesis and X-ray structure-function relationships of new coordination compounds for the application in molecular adsorption, luminescence, and optical sensor.

Filip Kielar received his Ph.D. in Chemistry from Durham University, UK in 2008. He has been working at Naresuan University, Thailand since 2012 where he holds the assistant professor position at the Department of Chemistry. His current research interests include luminescent transition metal complexes, biocatalysis, and applied optical spectroscopy.

Sarinya Hadsadee received her Ph.D. Chemistry from Ubon Ratchathani University, Thailand in 2019. Her research interests in calculation of organic dyes for dye-sensitized solar cells and calculation of theoretical investigation and molecular design of organic materials and catalyst.

Siriporn Jungsuttiwong received her Ph.D. in Physical Chemistry from Kasetsart University in 2005, Thailand. She is now working as a Professor in Physical Chemistry at the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ubon Ratchathani University, Thailand. Her current research interests include development of new catalysts/adsorbents for air treatment, new organic molecules for dye sensitized solar cells and transition metals decorated carbon nanocones on hydrogen storage reactions as well as new catalyst for conversion of light naphtha/CO2 to value-added product.

Sujittra Youngme received her Ph.D. in Inorganic Chemistry from the National University of Ireland at University College, Cork, Ireland. She is now working as a Professor in Inorganic Chemistry at the Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Khon Kaen University, Thailand. Her current research interests include design, syntheses, X-ray structure and functional properties of metal-organic frameworks.
